# Corporate Governance Reforms and Their Impact on Government Policies

![Writer: Directors' Institute]()

Corporate governance, the system of rules, practices, and processes by which companies are directed and controlled, is a critical aspect of the global business landscape. It has far-reaching implications for how businesses are run, how they interact with stakeholders, and how they contribute to the broader economy. Corporate governance, in essence, provides the framework through which a company's objectives are set and the means of attaining those objectives, as well as monitoring performance, are determined. In recent years, the topic of corporate governance reforms has gained significant attention, not only within the corporate sector but also in the corridors of government power.

This introduction sets the stage for an exploration of the profound impact that corporate governance reforms have had on government policies and regulations. Corporate governance reforms are at the heart of reshaping the relationship between the corporate world and the state, ushering in a transformation in government policies that touch various aspects of business operations and regulations.

Governments worldwide have recognized the need to reform and strengthen corporate governance practices to promote transparency, accountability, and responsible business conduct. The fallout from corporate scandals and financial crises in the early 21st century prompted governments to take action. These reforms are not isolated to a single country or region; they have become a global phenomenon, addressing issues that affect businesses, investors, and the public at large.

Corporate governance reforms influence government policies in several ways. They often result in changes to regulatory frameworks, corporate laws, and reporting requirements. These reforms aim to minimize conflicts of interest, protect shareholder rights, and ensure that corporate decision-making is in the best interest of the company and its stakeholders.

In the following sections, we will delve deeper into the multifaceted impact of corporate governance reforms on government policies, exploring topics such as enhanced regulatory frameworks, transparency and accountability standards, and the transformation of board and executive roles. We will also discuss how these reforms have shaped legal compliance and frameworks, as well as their effects on investor confidence and the stability of financial markets.

By examining the intricate interplay between corporate governance reforms and government policies, we can gain a better understanding of the evolving dynamics within the corporate world and its ever-growing influence on the broader socio-economic landscape.

![Corporate Governance Reforms](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/nsplsh_535b84e7cb304367b290764a8ea0296a~mv2.jpg/v1/fill/w_147,h_75,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/nsplsh_535b84e7cb304367b290764a8ea0296a~mv2.jpg)

## Corporate Governance Reforms: Enhanced Regulatory Framework

The realm of corporate governance is undergoing a profound transformation, reshaping the way companies operate and interact with the world. A central facet of this transformation is the quest for an enhanced regulatory framework. Corporate Governance Reforms, in their pursuit of accountability and transparency, have led to significant shifts in regulatory practices and government policies. In this section, we will explore the critical points that underscore the importance of an enhanced regulatory framework within the context of corporate governance reforms.

### Promoting Accountability:

One of the primary objectives of corporate governance reforms is to foster accountability. Enhanced regulatory frameworks play a pivotal role in holding corporations accountable for their actions. These regulations mandate increased disclosures, internal controls, and oversight mechanisms, ensuring that companies act in the best interest of their stakeholders. Through regulatory reforms, companies are compelled to adopt ethical business practices, thereby fortifying trust among investors and the public.

### Mitigating Risk:

In a world where global financial stability is paramount, an enhanced regulatory framework serves as a safeguard against systemic risks. The reforms necessitate stricter risk management practices and stress tests, reducing the likelihood of corporate failures that can have far-reaching consequences. These measures are designed to protect not only investors but also the broader economy, making regulatory enhancements a cornerstone in managing economic stability.

### Fostering Market Confidence:

Trust and confidence are the lifeblood of financial markets. An enhanced regulatory framework bolsters market confidence by setting clear rules and standards. Investors are more likely to participate in markets where they perceive a level playing field and where regulations assure transparency. Consequently, regulatory reforms not only contribute to better investor protection but also stimulate market activity, promoting economic growth.

The quest for an enhanced regulatory framework is an integral aspect of corporate governance reforms. It revolves around accountability, risk mitigation, and market confidence, all of which are pivotal to the stability and growth of modern economies. As we delve deeper into the impact of corporate governance reforms on government policies, this section demonstrates how regulatory enhancements are a driving force behind a more responsible and secure corporate landscape.

## Corporate Governance Reforms: Transparency and Accountability Standards

In the complex world of modern business, where corporations wield immense power and influence, the call for heightened transparency and accountability has grown louder. Corporate Governance Reforms have emerged as a powerful response to the imperatives of our times, reshaping the very core of how businesses are run and regulated. Central to these reforms are the principles of transparency and accountability, which are paramount in restoring faith in corporations and fortifying their bonds with stakeholders.

Restoring Trust: Transparency and accountability are the cornerstones of trust. In an environment marred by corporate scandals and breaches of fiduciary duty, businesses must open their operations to scrutiny. Corporate Governance Reforms demand greater transparency in financial reporting, decision-making processes, and stakeholder communication. This newfound openness helps in rebuilding trust, not only among shareholders but also with the wider public.

Empowering Stakeholders: In the modern corporate landscape, stakeholders extend far beyond just shareholders. Employees, customers, and the community at large are stakeholders too. Transparency and accountability standards acknowledge the rights and interests of these diverse groups. By providing them with a clear view of corporate operations and decision-making, reforms empower stakeholders to actively engage and hold businesses accountable.

Preventing Corporate Misconduct: Transparency and accountability serve as potent deterrents against corporate misconduct. Robust reporting requirements and disclosure rules ensure that unethical behavior is less likely to go unnoticed. This discourages corporate wrongdoings, making it increasingly difficult for executives to engage in fraudulent or unethical activities.

By adhering to these transparency and accountability standards, businesses not only conform to regulatory requirements but also pave the way for responsible corporate citizenship. Corporate Governance Reforms strive to strike a balance between fostering a competitive business environment and safeguarding the interests of all stakeholders. As we journey further into the realm of corporate governance reforms and their profound influence on government policies, it becomes evident that transparency and accountability standards are instrumental in shaping a business landscape characterized by integrity, trust, and sustainability.

## Corporate Governance Reforms: Shaping Board and Executive Roles

The landscape of corporate governance is undergoing a profound transformation, and at its core is the redefinition of board and executive roles. Corporate Governance Reforms are catalyzing this transformation, which is shaping the way companies are led and managed. This part goes into detail about how corporate governance reforms have changed these roles, pointing out the most important points that show how they have changed the way corporate leadership works.

### Balancing Power and Accountability:

One of the fundamental changes brought about by corporate governance reforms is the reconfiguration of the power dynamics within corporate boards. The goal of these reforms is to strike a balance between the executive board members' authority and the demands made of them in terms of accountability. The separation of CEO and Chairperson roles, for instance, is a direct outcome of these reforms, fostering a system of checks and balances that reduces the potential for conflicts of interest.

### Diversity and Inclusivity:

The evolving landscape of corporate governance emphasizes the importance of diversity in board and executive roles. Reforms increasingly mandate greater gender, ethnic, and skill diversity within these positions. By doing so, companies gain access to a broader range of perspectives, experiences, and expertise, which ultimately enhances decision-making and corporate performance.

### Fostering Long-Term Sustainability:

Corporate Governance Reforms encourage boards and executives to take a more long-term and sustainable view of their organizations. They emphasize the duty of loyalty to the corporation and its stakeholders, shifting the focus away from short-term gains to long-term value creation. These reforms, thus, encourage strategic thinking and planning that takes into account not just financial metrics but also social and environmental impacts.

The redefinition of board and executive roles is a cornerstone of corporate governance reforms. These reforms are reshaping power dynamics, fostering diversity and inclusivity, and encouraging a more sustainable approach to corporate leadership. As we go into more detail about how changes in corporate governance have affected government policies, this section stresses how these changes in leadership roles are a big part of making the business world more accountable, diverse, and forward-thinking.

## Corporate Governance Reforms: Impact on Investor Confidence

In an era defined by corporate scandals, economic crises, and increased stakeholder scrutiny, the demand for corporate governance reforms has surged. Among the most prominent objectives of these reforms is the establishment of transparency and accountability standards within organizations. This section explores the crucial roles that accountability and transparency play in corporate governance reforms, outlining the important points that highlight their significance in changing the corporate landscape.

Transparency and accountability are the cornerstones of trust. Businesses must expose their operations to scrutiny in a climate rife with corporate scandals and breaches of fiduciary duty. Corporate Governance Reforms demand greater transparency in financial reporting, decision-making processes, and stakeholder communication. This newfound openness helps in rebuilding trust, not only among shareholders but also with the wider public.

In the modern corporate landscape, stakeholders extend far beyond just shareholders. Employees, customers, and the community at large are stakeholders too. Transparency and accountability standards acknowledge the rights and interests of these diverse groups. By providing them with a clear view of corporate operations and decision-making, reforms empower stakeholders to actively engage and hold businesses accountable.

Transparency and accountability serve as potent deterrents against corporate misconduct. Robust reporting requirements and disclosure rules ensure that unethical behavior is less likely to go unnoticed. This discourages corporate wrongdoings, making it increasingly difficult for executives to engage in fraudulent or unethical activities.

By adhering to these transparency and accountability standards, businesses not only conform to regulatory requirements but also pave the way for responsible corporate citizenship. Corporate Governance Reforms strive to strike a balance between fostering a competitive business environment and safeguarding the interests of all stakeholders.

As we learn more about corporate governance reforms and how they have a big impact on government policies, it becomes clear that standards for openness and accountability play a big role in creating a business world that is honest, trustworthy, and long-lasting.

## END NOTE

The dynamic nature of corporate governance reforms and their significant impact on governmental policies indicate that these changes transcend mere regulatory adjustments. They represent a seismic shift in the relationship between the corporate world and the state, fostering an environment where transparency, accountability, and responsibility are paramount.

As corporations embrace these reforms, they navigate a new era where trust is rebuilt, stakeholder empowerment flourishes, and unethical behavior is discouraged. Simultaneously, governments worldwide adapt their policies to create a regulatory framework that encourages responsible corporate citizenship while safeguarding economic stability.

The impact of corporate governance reforms on government policies is a testament to the resilience and adaptability of our modern business landscape. These reforms transcend borders and industries, driving positive change and emphasizing the importance of sustainable, ethical, and accountable business practices.

In this intricate interplay between corporate governance reforms and government policies, we find the seeds of a more transparent, inclusive, and responsible corporate world. It is a journey that continues to shape the way businesses operate, inspiring confidence among investors, and contributing to the collective prosperity of society. As we move forward, it is imperative to recognize the vital role of these reforms in building a more just and sustainable global economy.

URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1061951824000089

SUMMARY:
We find that the implementation of corporate governance reforms in a country increases the propensity of firms in that country to issue earnings forecasts.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 3: REFORMS IN CORPORATE GOVERNANCE - TaxTMI ---
URL: https://www.taxtmi.com/article/detailed?id=641

SUMMARY:
Corporate governance boosts company performance and market trust ; emphasizes board roles, legal reforms, and accountability. Corporate governance deals with the rights and responsibilities of a company's management, its board shareholders and various stakeholders.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 4: Corporate governance reforms, societal trust ... - ScienceDirect.com ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566

SUMMARY:
Using a sample of 88,929 firm-year observations across 35 countries, we document a positive effect of corporate governance reforms on corporate financing and investment. Using a sample of firms from 35 countries during the period 1993–2012,4 we find support for the hypothesis that predicts a positive impact of corporate governance reforms on external financing and investment. For the moderating role of country-level trust, we find that the impact of corporate governance reforms on external financing and investment is stronger for firms domiciled in low-trust countries. As a result of the improvement in firm-level governance practices garnered from the adoption of corporate governance reforms, we expect that the impact of corporate governance reforms and trust on external financing and investment is stronger for more financially constrained firms and for firms with higher information asymmetry.

FULL CONTENT:

Highlights
----------

*   •We examine the joint effect of corporate governance reforms and societal trust on external financing and investment. 
*   •We document a positive effect of corporate governance reforms on firms' external financing and investment. 
*   •The positive effect is more pronounced for firms located in low-trust countries. 
*   •We highlight the substitutability between corporate governance and trust. 

Abstract
--------

While formal institutions are considered important in affecting corporate behaviors, little attention has been paid to the role of informal institutions in such relation. We employ the (formal) [corporate governance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/corporate-governance "Learn more about corporate governance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reforms and the (informal) individual countries' trust levels to examine how the informal institution moderates the impacts of the formal institution on [corporate financial policies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/managerial-finance "Learn more about corporate financial policies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Using a sample of 88,929 firm-year observations across 35 countries, we document a positive effect of corporate governance reforms on corporate financing and investment. More importantly, we find that the positive effect of the formal regulatory reforms is more pronounced with lower levels of informal country-level trust. Further analysis shows that corporate governance reforms lead to both lower under- and over-investment, and the effect is also stronger in low-trust countries. Our study sheds light on the interactive relation between corporate governance and trust and indicates that countries can offset the negative lack-of-trust effect on economic outcomes through formal regulatory reforms.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001542)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001554)

Keywords
--------

Corporate governance

Societal trust

Information asymmetry

External financing

Investment

> _A man I do not trust could not get money from me on all the bonds in Christendom._
> 
> _John Pierpont Morgan (1913)._

1. Introduction
---------------

Following the publication of the United Kingdom's Cadbury Report in 1992, many countries have implemented reforms to strengthen corporate governance and restore investors' confidence in the financial markets by imposing or recommending greater board independence, greater audit committee and [auditor independence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/audit-regulation "Learn more about auditor independence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and/or separation of the CEO and chairman of the board positions. These reforms have been the subject of a large and growing research because they constitute an exogenous shock to firms' internal governance practices and thus help circumvent the endogeneity concerns in empirical [finance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/finance "Learn more about finance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") literature ([Dahya and McConnell, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0145); [Kim and Lu, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0310); [Li, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0335); [Fauver et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0195); [Chen et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0125)). However, existing research either focuses on a single country and yields mixed results or ignores the influence of a country's informal norms on the effectiveness of adopting corporate governance reforms thus provides limited information for the evaluation of these reforms.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#fn0005)

In this paper, we examine whether and how a country's level of trust, as an informal institutional factor, influences the effectiveness of adopting corporate governance reforms. As defined by [Fukuyama (1995)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0215) and [Putnam (2000)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0395), societal trust is the expectations within a community that people will behave in honest and cooperative ways and the extent to which human interactions are governed by the norms of reciprocity and trustworthiness. Policymakers, theorists, and practitioners share the intuition that trust is fundamental to business growth and development of countries. For example, Mr. James Doty, the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB) Chairman pointed out that ‘as sophisticated as our markets and economy are, they are dependent on trust. We cannot take trust for granted’.[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#fn0010) Researchers also evince the critical role of trust in the interaction between managers and firm outsiders and document positive effects of trust on both macro- and firm-level outcomes ([Knack and Keefer, 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0320); [Zak and Knack, 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0485); [Guiso et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0240); [Pevzner et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0390); [Dudley and Zhang, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0185)). Given that trust is indispensable to the well-functioning of markets and success of economies, especially when implementing regulatory reforms, it is important to understand whether and how country-level societal trust affects such formal regulatory reforms as well as their impacts on corporate decisions.

To investigate this issue, we begin with an examination of the impact of corporate governance reforms on firms' financing and investment decisions. We then condition this relation on country-level societal trust. The focus on firms' financing and investment decisions is important because they are two key topics in finance ([Rauh, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0405); [Derrien and KecskÉS, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0155)) and have material effect on firm value, hence economic growth ([Rajan and Zingales, 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0400); [Tirole, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0465)).[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#fn0015) Also, firms' financing and investment decisions align well with trust between managers and outside investors (as highlighted in the opening quote) and thus provide an ideal setting to examine the interaction between corporate governance and trust. In the neoclassical framework, financing is irrelevant as investment opportunities are the only determinant of investment ([Modigliani and Miller, 1958](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0360); [Abel, 1983](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0005)). However, extant literature documents that market frictions, such as [information asymmetry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/information-asymmetry "Learn more about information asymmetry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), exist and can generate financing frictions, affecting firms' investment decisions ([Hubbard, 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0300); [Stein, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0450)). For instance, an information asymmetry between managers and investors leads to a higher cost of external financing, resulting in a firm's propensity to reject a positive net [present value](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/present-value "Learn more about present value from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (NPV) project ([Jensen and Meckling, 1976](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0305); [Myers and Majluf, 1984](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0365)). Consequently, several studies suggest that factors that decrease information asymmetry should lead to an increase in financing and investment (see [Roychowdhury et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0420) for a review). Since the adoption of corporate governance reform serves as a positive shock to quality firm-level governance practices and helps to curb managers' opportunistic behaviors and improves integrity and transparency of financial reporting ([Dahya and McConnell, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0145); [Armstrong et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0040); [Fauver et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0195)), it follows that there should be a positive relation between corporate governance reforms and firms' financing and investment. Therefore, we expect a positive impact of corporate governance reforms on external financing and investment.

Then, we examine the moderating role of country-level trust, which, ex ante, is not unambiguous. On the one hand, trust is recognized for serving an informal institution in protecting shareholders' rights and mitigating agency problems ([Jensen and Meckling, 1976](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0305); [Williamson, 1993](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0475)). Prior studies suggest that trust can solve the problem of opportunism and moral hazard, as does corporate governance, and thus can moderates the use of formal corporate governance mechanisms ([Carlin et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0100); [Aghion et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0020); [Christensen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0140)). The interactive relation between trust and corporate governance is consistent with the arguments in prior studies ([Knack and Keefer, 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0320); [Licht et al., 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0345)). [Knack and Keefer (1997)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0320), for example, document that investors in more trustworthy societies devote fewer resources to ensuring that they are not exploited in a transaction. Accordingly, one could expect that corporate governance is valued higher in an environment with lower trust, and a more significant impact of corporate governance reforms on external financing and investment will be observed in countries with a lower societal trust.

On the other hand, it is possible that trust and corporate governance are complementing in influencing firms' financing and investment decisions. Social trust, or more broadly embedded social norms and beliefs, are ingrained, stable and resistant to change ([Williamson, 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0480); [Tabellini, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0460)), and thus regulations are likely a reflection of a broader societal stance. [Arrow (1974)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0050) also indicates that trust reflects not only the propensity to honor obligations, but also the way in which people take pride in the formal laws and regulations of their country. From this perspective, countries characterized by a high societal trust will exhibit high trust in the efficacy and credibility of the reforms, while countries characterized by a low societal trust will exhibit a certain dose of skepticism and reservation to the reforms, suggesting that the adoption of corporate governance reforms will exert a greater impact in countries with a higher societal trust.

Using a sample of firms from 35 countries during the period 1993–2012,[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#fn0020) we find support for the hypothesis that predicts a positive impact of corporate governance reforms on external financing and investment. Specifically, we observe that firms raise additional external financing and increase investment following the adoption of corporate governance reforms. Our estimates indicate that firms on average raise additional external capital equal to 4.4% of assets and increase investment equal to 1.1% of assets in the post-regulation period. To ensure the validity of our difference-in-differences (DiD) analysis, we check whether our results are driven by the violation of the parallel trend assumption in the pre-regulation period. We find that the effect of the governance reforms on external financing and investment is nonsignificant in the pre-regulation period and only becomes significant in the year of the reform implementation and thereafter (consistent with the parallel trend assumption). Our results thus provide support of a causal effect of corporate governance reforms on external financing and investment.

For the moderating role of country-level trust, we find that the impact of corporate governance reforms on external financing and investment is stronger for firms domiciled in low-trust countries. In particular, we find that firms domiciled in low-trust countries increase external financing by 1.7% of assets and increase investment by 1.1% of assets, on average, more than those in high-trust countries. We address several potential challenges to confirm the role of trust on the impact of corporate governance reforms, including using alternative specifications such as a stacked DiD estimation, using alternative measures of external financing and investment, using alternative measures of trust, and controlling for several country level traits. Our findings support the argument that improvements in firm-level governance reduce information asymmetry and lower monitoring costs, leading to easier access to external capital and facilitating investment, and the effect is amplified for firms in low-trust countries.

We conduct four sets of supplementary tests to provide additional support to our findings. First, we consider the role of financial constraints and information opacity. Financially constrained firms and firms characterized with poor information quality tend to have greater financing needs, but, at the same time, their information asymmetry make them difficult to raise capital, forcing them to forgo profitable opportunities. As a result of the improvement in firm-level governance practices garnered from the adoption of corporate governance reforms, we expect that the impact of corporate governance reforms and trust on external financing and investment is stronger for more financially constrained firms and for firms with higher information asymmetry. Our empirical findings support this view and confirm that governance reforms reduce information asymmetry.

Second, we examine the effect of corporate governance reforms on investment efficiency following the methodology of [Biddle et al. (2009)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0075). As improved board monitoring and corporate transparency following the reforms allow firms to raise external capital, they can reduce the under-investment problem. Similarly, improved board monitoring and reduced information asymmetry garnered from the adoption of corporate governance reforms can help mitigate managers' wasteful spending to mitigate over-investment. For instance, the board can take timely corrective action to prevent investments in negative NPV projects, which should mitigate over-investment and improve corporate investment efficiency. The results of our analysis lend support to this prediction, as reflected in lower under- and over-investment, in the post-reform period. We also find that the effect is stronger for firms in low-trust countries.[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#fn0025),[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#fn0030)

Third, we examine and find that reform promoting greater board independence, which seeks to strengthen board's oversight of corporate insiders ([Fama and Jensen, 1983](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0190)), and reform promoting greater audit committee and auditor independence, which seeks to enhance the integrity and transparency of corporate financial reporting ([Klein, 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0315)), increase external financing and investment, especially for firms in low-trust countries. These results suggest that increased financial transparency and monitoring effectiveness brought about by the adoption of governance reforms account for our findings, with trust moderating the effect.

Finally, we investigate the effect of corporate governance reforms on the cost of debt. Previous studies highlight the role of agency-related frictions for the costly nature of external financing ([Jensen and Meckling, 1976](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0305); [Myers and Majluf, 1984](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0365)). We reason that the reforms address the agency cost of debt by strengthening firm-level governance practices and reducing information asymmetry, and thus should reduce the cost of debt financing. Consistent with our prediction, we find that the adoption of corporate governance reforms is negatively associated with cost of debt and the effect is amplified for firms in low-trust countries. Overall, the results of these additional tests corroborate our baseline findings that the adoption of corporate governance reforms eases firms' access to external financing and facilitates efficient investment, especially for firms in low-trust countries.

Our study makes several important contributions. First, we contribute to the literature on corporate governance reforms and corporate policies. Recent studies explore the effects of corporate governance reforms on various corporate outcomes including firm value ([Fauver et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0195)), cash holdings ([Chen et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0125)), dividend payment ([Bae et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0055)), and IPO underpricing ([Chen et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0130)). We add to this growing literature by documenting a positive impact of corporate governance reforms on external financing and investment. We also show that the adoption of corporate governance reforms leads to investment efficiency, as evident by mitigation of both over-investment and under-investment. Our study is also contributory to a classic line of research in corporate finance documenting the impact of major reforms and financial shocks on financing and investment ([Henry, 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0275); [Derrien and KecskÉS, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0155); [Petacchi, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0385)) and revealing how the supply of capital affects investment ([Fazzari et al., 1988](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0200); [Hoshi et al., 1991](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0285)). Furthermore, we contribute to the literature examining the heterogeneity of firms' financing and investment decisions in a global setting ([Shroff, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0435)).

Second, we provide empirical evidence on an important topic that has been possibly overlooked in prior studies, namely the interplay between corporate governance and societal trust. Although both corporate governance and trust have been shown to moderate manager-investor relations and drive performance ([Fauver et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0195); [Lins et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0355)), little is known about how they work together to determine corporate behavior. Our study helps fill this void by testing this interaction in the context of corporate governance reforms and trust. We document evidence of substitutability between corporate governance and societal trust by showing that the positive effect of corporate governance reforms on external financing and investment is more pronounced with lower country-level trust. This is an important finding as it highlights a condition under which government intervention is optimal and could provide an important reference for policy makers in maintaining the proper function of the capital markets and in achieving the desired economic growth. In particular, countries with lower trust can benefit more from the adoption of corporate governance reforms, suggesting that effective corporate governance can compensate for the negative effect of lack-of-trust on economic outcomes. Previous research shows that a lack of trust reduces stock market participation, trade and investment which affects economic growth ([Knack and Keefer, 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0320); [Zak and Knack, 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0485); [Guiso et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0240), [Guiso et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0245); [Smith, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0440); [Giannetti and Wang, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0929119923001566#bb0220)). Our evidence suggests that the adoption of corporate governance reforms can be a viable way for low-trust countries to increase investor confidence in the stock market and alleviate the negative effect of

enriched_points.expanded_content: Corporate governance reforms enhance accountability, transparency, and board effectiveness, fostering investor confidence and improving corporate performance across various sectors.
Continuous governance reforms are essential for adapting to market changes, promoting ethical practices, and maintaining investor trust amidst evolving regulatory landscapes.
Organizations must actively engage in governance reforms, ensuring compliance and transparency to strengthen stakeholder relationships and enhance overall corporate reputation.
Businesses must be agile, adapting governance practices to emerging trends and regulatory changes to remain competitive and mitigate risks effectively.
A strong commitment to ethical practices is vital for fostering cultural integrity within organizations, building long-term trust among stakeholders, and contributing to sustainable success.
